source_evidence: Eq. (5)-(6): A_2 = A_1 A_1 = [A_1^2]; [A_n]_{p,q} = 0 if [A_1^n]_{p,q}=0 or p=q, 1 if [A_1^n]_{p,q}>0 and p!=q. New bars for level n: G_n = A_n - A_{n-1}, with G_1 = A_1.
purpose: Generates higher connectivity-level nodal adjacency by matrix powering of the level-1 connectivity, then isolates the new candidate bars introduced at each level to control ground structure redundancy.
assumptions: Level n connectivity reaches neighbors-of-neighbors up to n hops
Off-diagonal positive entries of A_1^n are collapsed to boolean true
Process decelerates only when member generation reaches full connectivity
High connectivity levels are not recommended (long members poorly approximate curved optimal members)
formula_or_algorithm: Compute A_1^n by repeated matrix multiplication. Booleanize: [A_n]_{p,q} = 1 if [A_1^n]_{p,q} > 0 and p != q else 0. New bars at level n: G_n = A_n - A_{n-1} (G_1 = A_1). Non-zero entries of G_n are candidate bars for that level; they still must pass collinearity and restriction-zone checks. Only bars with p < q are reported to avoid duplicates.
validation_opportunities: unit test comparing level-1/2/3 bar counts on the 2-element mesh (Fig. 2)
monotonic growth of N_b with level (Tables 2-8)

reuse_risk.explanation: Concept is clear but matrix powers scale rapidly in 3D and can exhaust memory; booleanization and the G_n differencing must be applied carefully to avoid duplicate or spurious bars.

missing_information: Memory management for dense powers at high levels
Exact deduplication and p<q reporting logic in code
